race_name: Copper Triangle — Colorado's Three-Pass Alpine Loop

top_categories.workout_context: 6,500ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
82ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Copper Triangle — Colorado's Three-Pass Alpine Loop's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before August.
79 miles on paved mountain highways and dedicated bike paths burns energy faster than the distance suggests. Train the fade resistance now.
At Copper Triangle — Colorado's Three-Pass Alpine Loop, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Copper Triangle — Colorado's Three-Pass Alpine Loop. Train it until it feels boring.
Base fitness for Copper Triangle — Colorado's Three-Pass Alpine Loop. Without this, the hard workouts break you down instead of building you up.

Copper Triangle — Colorado's Three-Pass Alpine Loop's paved mountain highways and dedicated bike paths forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 79 miles.
6,500ft of climbing on grades that change every minute. You need to switch from seated to standing without losing rhythm.
Double-threshold training builds sustained power with less fatigue. More FTP per hour of training.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 6,500ft of climbing on paved mountain highways and dedicated bike paths does to your power file on race day.
76–85% FTP for extended efforts. At Copper Triangle — Colorado's Three-Pass Alpine Loop, tempo is the floor you'll live on. Make it feel easy.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 40–60g carbs/hour for Copper Triangle — Colorado's Three-Pass Alpine Loop’s 79 miles. Front-load calories in the first half. One bottle per hour minimum, more in heat.
race_overlay.altitude: Copper Triangle — Colorado's Three-Pass Alpine Loop with 6,500ft of climbing, much of it above 8,000ft demands altitude preparation. Arrive 5–7 days early for acclimatization. Expect 5–15% power reduction at altitude. Increase iron intake 4 weeks out. Hydrate aggressively — altitude increases fluid loss by 20–40%. Entire route above 9,000 ft — relentless altitude with no low-elevation recovery; Temperature swings from cool dawn to warm valleys to cold pass summits.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on VO2max, TT Threshold, and Race Simulation to prepare you for 79 miles of paved mountain highways and dedicated bike paths in Copper Mountain Resort, Colorado.
